title: Can Lightning Protection Systems Help Trees in Florida?
strengths: Correctly frames lightning protection as a specialized preservation tool rather than immunity; identifies specimen, historic, sentimental, and high-value tree candidates; notes that structural condition and ongoing care still matter.
weaknesses: No H1, sources, decision table, internal links, service route, standards context, installer qualification, grounding design, inspection cycle, post-strike inspection path, or separation from building lightning protection; repetitive value-based rhetoric.
ai_notes: Short Answer followed by candidate lists, when it makes sense, when it does not, common mistakes, professional guidance, and final takeaway.
florida_notes: Florida lightning exposure is relevant, but the article needs a tree-specific system-design and maintenance boundary. It should not imply that an ordinary dispatch decision can establish candidacy or protection performance.
internal_link_opportunities: lightning-struck tree, lightning-strike removal timing, cracks in bark, high-value tree preservation, cabling/bracing, tree-risk assessment.
cta_recommendation: Route first to qualified tree lightning-protection assessment and installation; use tree-service links only for compatible pruning, post-strike cleanup, or removal.

ai_notes: Short Answer, tool definition, root-pruning explanation, scenarios, when it helps, when it does not, better questions, mistakes, guidance, and takeaway.
florida_notes: Air excavation may be used for diagnosis without pruning. Root pruning, root-collar excavation, soil work, utility exposure, and construction planning need distinct scopes and qualified oversight.
